# Welcome to Mikayla Humphrey's COMP110 Website

<img src="{{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/logo.png" alt="Comp110 logo" width="500"/>

## About this Project

This website involves the data analysis I completed in order to determine ways to further improve the COMP110 course in the upcoming years. It consists of the process of how I chose my idea to analyze, the three visualizations I created, and a detailed reflection of what the visualizations showcased in regards to my idea and future improvements.

### Choosing my Analysis

Idea to analyze with available data: Idea 2 - The course should implement more psychology/neuroscience examples when coding in order to peak the interest of students in those majors.

This idea is more valuable than the others brainstormed because: I am able to analyze the data to see if my idea is supported because majors and interest levels were collected in the survey. I will be able to determine how interested psychology/neuroscience majors were in this course and see if there are ways to increase their interest levels in the upcoming years.

### Visualizations

#### Visualization 1: Distribution Plot of Student Understanding

This plot shows the distribution of student understanding ratings from the survey.

![Visualization 1]({{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/viz1.png)

#### Visualization 2: Box Plot Comparing Interest Ratings Across Different Majors

This box plot compares interest ratings across different majors.

![Visualization 2]({{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/viz2.png)

#### Visualization 3: Scatter Plot Showing Relationship Between Difficulty and Value

This scatter plot shows the relationship between perceived difficulty and value of the course.

![Visualization 3]({{ site.baseurl }}/static/imgs/viz3.png)

### Conclusion

When looking at the visualizations I created, there were three different comparisons that were made. The first involves the overall distribution of the understanding rates of the content in the class. The second involves the interest rates of the class based on specific majors. Finally, the third involves a three way comparison between course value, course difficulty, and overall understanding. 

The second visualization I made best reflects the idea I suggested to better improve the course. When looking at the visualization, I can see the average interest ratings for the students in each major. By looking at the diagram, I can see that those who are majoring in psychology/neuroscience typically had slightly lower interest ratings for the class compared to other majors. The ratings averaged around a 3 for psychology majors and averaged around a 4 for neuroscience majors. There were other majors that had much higher ratings, such as statistics/analytics and mathematics. This is not a surprising discovery, considering this course tends to focus on content that involves more calculations and computer work. However, I do think it would be beneficial to add some components of other majors into this course, especially because it is an introduction level course where there is more room to explore. While it is still important to teach basic concepts of computer science, implementing different elements of psychology/neuroscience into the course could make it more engaging. For example, when coding data and cleaning it, adding some psychology/neuroscience terminology could capture the attention of students in these majors. Additionally, implementing spefific psychological learning concepts into the course could make it more engaging for everyone involved. Making coding exercises shorter, making assignments more like games (having progress bars and badges when sections are completed), and having less lecture time and more active coding time in class could overall make the class more engaging and make the material more successfully retained among students, regardless of what major they are in.

While my other visualizations don't reflect the idea I presented, I do think they are important to still discuss. In my first visualization, it is clear that the most common rating of understanding that came from the student survey was a 5, followed by a 4 and a 6. Considering this question was on a 7-point scale, this survey question reflects lots of success in the course. This course is not easy, and is a big learning curve for new programmers. However, this survey response does show that the material was taught in such a way that students were mostly understanding the material, despite the difficult aspects of it. In my third visualization, there are a few relationships to look at. Based on the visual, it appears that the less students understood the material, the harder they felt the class was and the less value they felt they got out of the class overall. This is an important analysis, as it is very possible that increasing the interest level of students in this course could also potentially help them better understand the material and get more value out of the course. Everything works together in some way, and there are always ways to improve everything in life. This course was challenging and was difficult to follow at times, but I do feel that Professor Hinks did a great job explaining the content in each lecture we had, no matter how complex it was.
